preamble: The shift from the bilabial stop [p] to the velar stop [k] is known as velarization or revelation. It is a rare shift that typically occurs as part of a chain shift or through 'grave' consonant substitution, where two sounds at opposite ends of the mouth (lips and velum) are acoustically confused due to their similar low-frequency resonance.

examples.note: Hawaiian shifted alveolars to velars after the original velars had become glottal stops, demonstrating the language's tendency toward rear articulation.
While the shift was [kʷ] to [p], the resulting doublets across the family established a strong historical and phonetic link between the labial and velar positions.
